runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 8 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Gruber and Saez (2002) - Elasticity of Taxable Income
Congressional Budget Office (CBO) microsimulation parameters
Immervoll et al. (2007) - EUROMOD-style tax-benefit microsimulation elasticities
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners in the upper-middle part of the earnings distribution (decile 8) typically have moderate substitution elasticities. The labor supply literature generally finds primary earner (often male) elasticities in the range of 0.1–0.4 on the intensive margin. Decile 8 earners are high but not top earners, so their behavioral response to marginal wage changes is moderate. Tax-benefit microsimulation models like those used by CBO or in EUROMOD-style frameworks commonly calibrate substitution elasticities for primary earners in this range around 0.15–0.25. Gruber and Saez's taxable income elasticity estimates and related work anchor the central tendency around 0.2, with somewhat higher values plausible if the model incorporates broader behavioral margins. I center my estimate at 0.2 with moderate uncertainty reflecting variation across model specifications and identification strategies.

(2007) - EUROMOD-style tax-benefit microsimulation elasticities"], "reasoning_summary": "Primary earners in the upper-middle part of the earnings distribution (decile 8) typically have moderate substitution elasticities. Tax-benefit microsimulation models like those used by CBO or in EUROMOD-style frameworks commonly calibrate substitution elasticities for primary earners in this range around 0.15\u20130.25. Gruber and Saez's taxable income elasticity estimates and related work anchor the central tendency around 0.2, with somewhat higher values plausible if the model incorporates broader behavioral margins. I center my estimate at 0.2 with moderate uncertainty reflecting variation across model specifications and identification strategies."}

runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners typically have low labor supply elasticities. In U.S. tax-benefit microsimulations (e.g., CBO's models), substitution elasticities for primary earners in upper-middle deciles like decile 8 are usually in the range of 0.1–0.3. These workers are mostly full-time with limited hours flexibility, so their compensated elasticity is modest. Gruber & Saez found taxable income elasticities around 0.12–0.40 depending on specification; CBO uses substitution elasticities around 0.15–0.25 for primary earners. Decile 8 earners are upper-middle income, not at the top where behavioral responses may differ. I center my estimate at 0.20, with moderate uncertainty spanning roughly 0.05 to 0.45.

I center my estimate at 0.20 with moderate uncertainty reflecting variation across model specifications and empirical estimates.

runs.reasoningSummary: For primary earners (typically men working full-time), substitution elasticities are generally modest. In U.S. tax-benefit microsimulations (e.g., CBO-style models), calibrated substitution elasticities for primary earners in this range typically fall around 0.15–0.25. I center my estimate at 0.2, with moderate uncertainty reflecting variation across model specifications and empirical estimates (Gruber-Saez taxable income elasticities, structural labor supply models). The range 0.05–0.45 captures plausible extremes from near-zero behavioral response to higher values used in some simulation exercises.
